batchName: Advanced Java — Concurrency, Streams & Modern Features

questions.question: What does the volatile keyword guarantee in Java, and what does it NOT guarantee?
questions.quickAnswer: volatile guarantees visibility (reads/writes go directly to main memory, not CPU cache) and prevents instruction reordering around the variable. It does NOT guarantee atomicity — `volatile int i; i++` is still a read-modify-write race. Use AtomicInteger for compound operations.
keyPoints: Visibility: every thread sees the latest write immediately
Happens-before: write to volatile happens-before subsequent read
NOT atomic: i++ is three operations (read, increment, write)
Good for: flags (boolean stop = false), double-checked locking DCL pattern
Bad for: counters, check-then-act compound actions

questions.question: When would you use ReentrantLock over synchronized, and what extra capabilities does it offer?
questions.quickAnswer: ReentrantLock offers tryLock (non-blocking attempt), lockInterruptibly (cancel waiting thread), fairness policy (longest-waiting gets lock first), and multiple Condition queues. Use synchronized for simple critical sections; ReentrantLock when you need timeout, interruptibility, or multiple wait-sets.
keyPoints: tryLock(timeout) — avoid indefinite blocking
lockInterruptibly() — thread.interrupt() can unblock waiting thread
Fair mode — prevents starvation (but lower throughput)
Multiple Conditions — producer/consumer with separate await/signal queues
MUST unlock in finally block — unlike synchronized, no auto-release

questions.question: What is ThreadLocal, and when can it cause memory leaks?
questions.quickAnswer: ThreadLocal stores a per-thread value (e.g., DB connection, user context) without passing it through method parameters. Memory leak risk: in thread pools (Tomcat, HikariCP), threads live forever. If you set a ThreadLocal and never remove it, the value stays in memory for the thread's lifetime. Always call remove() in a finally block.
keyPoints: Each thread gets its own independent copy — no synchronization needed
Common use: Spring SecurityContextHolder, MDC logging context, SimpleDateFormat
Leak: pooled threads + strong reference in ThreadLocalMap = GC cannot collect
Fix: threadLocal.remove() in finally, or use try-with-resources wrapper
InheritableThreadLocal passes values to child threads (e.g., request ID to async tasks)

questions.question: Explain the Java Memory Model's happens-before relationship. Why does it matter?
questions.quickAnswer: Happens-before is a guarantee that memory writes by one operation are visible to another. Without it, the JVM and CPU can reorder instructions for performance. Key rules: unlock happens-before subsequent lock of same monitor; volatile write happens-before subsequent volatile read; Thread.start() happens-before the started thread's actions.
keyPoints: Program order: each action in a thread HB subsequent actions in same thread
Monitor: unlock HB lock (synchronized guarantees visibility on exit/entry)
Volatile: write HB read (hence volatile fixes DCL)
Thread.start(): all actions before start() are visible in the new thread
Thread.join(): all actions in thread T are visible after T.join() returns

questions.question: How does ConcurrentHashMap differ from HashMap and Hashtable?
questions.quickAnswer: HashMap is not thread-safe (concurrent modification can cause infinite loop in Java 7). Hashtable is thread-safe via synchronized on every method (coarse-grained, very slow). ConcurrentHashMap uses segment-level locking (Java 7) or CAS + bin-level synchronization (Java 8+) for fine-grained concurrency — reads are mostly lock-free.
keyPoints: Java 8: buckets use CAS for inserts, synchronized only on bin (linked list/tree) during resize
computeIfAbsent is atomic — safe for cache-aside pattern
size() returns approximate count (not locked) — use mappingCount() for large maps
Does not allow null keys or values (unlike HashMap)
Iterators are weakly consistent — reflect state at or after creation, don't throw CME

questions.question: What is a deadlock, and how do you prevent it in Java?
questions.quickAnswer: Deadlock: thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2; thread B holds lock 2 and waits for lock 1 — both wait forever. Prevention: always acquire multiple locks in the same global order; use tryLock with timeout; use a single coarser lock; or use lock-free data structures.
keyPoints: Four conditions (Coffman): mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
Break circular wait: define total ordering on locks (e.g., by System.identityHashCode)
tryLock(timeout): if can't get second lock, release first and retry
Thread dump analysis: jstack, VisualVM, or thread dump shows 'BLOCKED' + lock chain
Live-lock: threads keep releasing and re-acquiring — use back-off with jitter

questions.question: What are the different types of ExecutorService thread pools and when do you choose each?
questions.quickAnswer: newFixedThreadPool(n): bounded pool, predictable resource use — best for CPU-bound tasks (n = # cores). newCachedThreadPool: unbounded, creates threads on demand — for short-lived IO tasks (can OOM under load). newSingleThreadExecutor: one thread, tasks run sequentially. newScheduledThreadPool: periodic/delayed tasks. For production, prefer custom ThreadPoolExecutor with bounded queue.
keyPoints: newFixedThreadPool: LinkedBlockingQueue (unbounded!) — tasks queue indefinitely, can OOM
newCachedThreadPool: SynchronousQueue — no buffering, thread created per task
Always name threads (ThreadFactory) for readable thread dumps
RejectedExecutionHandler: AbortPolicy (default), CallerRunsPolicy (backpressure), DiscardPolicy
Virtual threads (Java 21): replace cached pools for IO-bound tasks

questions.question: How does CompletableFuture work, and how do you handle exceptions in async pipelines?
questions.quickAnswer: CompletableFuture lets you chain async computations: thenApply (map result), thenCompose (flatMap/chain), thenCombine (merge two futures). For exceptions: exceptionally(fn) recovers by returning a fallback; handle(fn) receives both result and exception (always called); whenComplete for side effects without altering result.
keyPoints: thenApply: synchronous transform (Function<T,U>)
thenApplyAsync: runs transform on ForkJoinPool.commonPool() or custom executor
thenCompose: chains futures (avoids CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<T>>)
exceptionally: converts exception to a fallback value — breaks the error chain
allOf / anyOf: wait for all / any of a list of futures

questions.question: What is the difference between CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, and Semaphore?
questions.quickAnswer: CountDownLatch: one-time gate — one or more threads wait until countdown reaches zero (e.g., wait for 3 services to init). CyclicBarrier: reusable rendezvous — N threads wait at a barrier until all arrive, then all proceed (e.g., parallel batch rounds). Semaphore: controls access count — limits concurrent access to a resource (e.g., max 10 DB connections).
keyPoints: CountDownLatch: count never resets — single use only
CyclicBarrier: resets after each round, optional runnable runs after each barrier break
Semaphore: acquire() blocks when 0 permits, release() returns a permit
Semaphore(1) ≈ mutex, but not reentrant and not owned by a thread
Phaser: flexible generalization of both Latch and Barrier — supports dynamic party registration

questions.question: How does ForkJoinPool differ from a standard thread pool, and what is work stealing?
questions.quickAnswer: ForkJoinPool is designed for divide-and-conquer tasks. Each worker thread has its own deque. When a thread finishes its tasks, it steals from the tail of another thread's deque — keeping all threads busy. Standard pools have one shared queue with contention. ForkJoinPool.commonPool() is used by parallel streams and CompletableFuture by default.
keyPoints: ForkJoinTask: fork() submits subtask asynchronously; join() waits for result
RecursiveTask<V>: returns a value; RecursiveAction: no return value
Work stealing reduces idle time — especially good for unbalanced subtasks
commonPool size = Runtime.availableProcessors() - 1
Avoid blocking IO in ForkJoinPool — starves other tasks; use ManagedBlocker

questions.question: What are functional interfaces, and how does Java resolve ambiguity with method references?
questions.quickAnswer: A functional interface has exactly one abstract method (@FunctionalInterface annotation is optional but documents intent). Common ones: Function<T,R>, Predicate<T>, Consumer<T>, Supplier<T>, BiFunction<T,U,R>. Method references (Class::method) are syntactic sugar for lambdas; Java resolves which functional interface to use from the target type context.
keyPoints: static ref: Integer::parseInt → Function<String, Integer>
instance ref on type: String::toUpperCase → Function<String, String>
instance ref on instance: myList::add → Consumer<T>
constructor ref: ArrayList::new → Supplier<List>
Ambiguity: if a method is both static and instance-on-type, compiler error — be explicit

questions.question: How is Stream lazy evaluation implemented, and when does it cause bugs?
questions.quickAnswer: Intermediate stream operations (filter, map, flatMap) are lazy — they return a new Stream descriptor without executing. Terminal operations (collect, forEach, count) trigger the pipeline. Bug: capturing a mutable variable in a lambda; the lambda runs at terminal time, not at the line where it's defined — so the variable's value at execution may differ.
keyPoints: Lazy = short-circuit optimization: filter+findFirst stops at first match
flatMap is lazy in Java 16+ (was eagerly evaluated before)
Closed stream: a stream can only be consumed once — terminal op closes it
Stateful intermediate ops (sorted, distinct) buffer — can't be lazy end-to-end
Side effects in map/filter are dangerous in parallel streams — use forEach carefully

questions.question: What are the pitfalls of parallel streams, and when should you NOT use them?
questions.quickAnswer: Parallel streams split work across ForkJoinPool.commonPool() threads. Pitfalls: shared mutable state causes races; IO-bound tasks block pool threads (starving other tasks); overhead of splitting/merging often exceeds benefit for small lists (<10k elements). Use parallel streams only for CPU-bound, stateless, non-IO work on large collections.
keyPoints: Shared state: list.parallelStream().forEach(list::add) — data race, ConcurrentModificationException
Ordered ops: findFirst, limit are slow in parallel — lose ordering guarantee
Splitting cost: LinkedList splits O(n) — ArrayList/arrays split O(1)
Common pool contention: your parallel stream competes with FJ pool users globally
Custom pool: new ForkJoinPool(n).submit(() -> list.parallelStream().collect(...))

questions.question: How does Optional prevent NullPointerException, and what are its anti-patterns?
questions.quickAnswer: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not hold a value, forcing callers to handle the absent case. Use orElse, orElseGet, ifPresent, map, flatMap, or. Anti-patterns: optional.get() without isPresent() (same NPE risk), using Optional as method parameter or field (adds overhead, breaks serialization), wrapping collections (empty list is better than Optional<List>).
keyPoints: orElse(default): always evaluates default (even if expensive) — prefer orElseGet(() -> expensive())
map: transforms value if present, returns Optional.empty() if absent
flatMap: use when transformation itself returns Optional (avoid Optional<Optional<T>>)
ifPresentOrElse (Java 9): both branches in one call
Stream integration: optional.stream() returns 0 or 1 element

questions.question: What are default and static methods on interfaces, and how does Java resolve diamond inheritance?
questions.quickAnswer: Default methods (Java 8) let interfaces provide a method body, enabling backward-compatible API evolution. Static interface methods are utility methods scoped to the interface. Diamond resolution: class implementation wins over interface default; if two interfaces provide the same default, the implementing class MUST override (compile error otherwise).
keyPoints: Default enables adding methods to existing interfaces without breaking implementations
Class > interface: if a class and interface both provide the method, class wins
Interface A > interface B: if B extends A and both have the default, B's wins
Conflict: two unrelated interfaces, same signature — must override in implementing class
Explicitly calling a specific default: InterfaceA.super.method()

questions.question: What are sealed classes (Java 17), and how do they enable exhaustive pattern matching?
questions.quickAnswer: Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend them (permits clause). Combined with switch pattern matching (Java 21), the compiler can verify all subtype cases are handled without a default branch. This is the foundation of Algebraic Data Types in Java — safe, exhaustive modeling of closed hierarchies.
keyPoints: sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle { }
Permitted subclasses must be in the same package/module
Subclasses must be: final, sealed, or non-sealed
Switch expression with guarded patterns: case Circle c when c.radius() > 10
Enables compiler-checked exhaustiveness — no silent runtime gaps

questions.question: What are virtual threads (Java 21), and how do they change the threading model?
questions.quickAnswer: Virtual threads are lightweight JVM-managed threads (not OS threads). The JVM scheduler mounts virtual threads onto a small pool of carrier (OS) threads. When a virtual thread blocks on IO, it is unmounted from the carrier (which picks up another virtual thread). This gives the simplicity of thread-per-request with the scalability of async IO — no callback hell.
keyPoints: Thread.ofVirtual().start(task) or Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor()
Carrier pool = # CPU cores (ForkJoinPool) — not # virtual threads
Pinning: synchronized block holds the carrier (use ReentrantLock instead for IO-heavy code)
Stack: virtual thread stacks are heap-allocated and grow/shrink; no 512KB OS stack waste
Not for CPU-bound: parallel computation still needs platform threads + ForkJoinPool

questions.question: What is AtomicInteger and how does CAS (Compare-And-Swap) work internally?
questions.quickAnswer: AtomicInteger wraps an int with lock-free thread-safe operations using CPU-level CAS instructions. CAS(expected, update): atomically checks if current value == expected; if yes, sets it to update and returns true; if no (another thread changed it), returns false. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() loops until CAS succeeds — no lock needed.
keyPoints: CAS is a single hardware instruction (CMPXCHG on x86) — cannot be interrupted
compareAndSet(expected, update) — returns boolean
getAndUpdate(fn), updateAndGet(fn) — lambda-based atomic transforms
Spurious failure: CAS may fail even without contention on RISC — Java handles retry loop
LongAdder: better than AtomicLong under high contention — striped counters, reduce CAS retry

questions.question: What is BlockingQueue and how does it support producer-consumer patterns?
questions.quickAnswer: BlockingQueue is a thread-safe queue where put() blocks when full and take() blocks when empty — built-in coordination. ArrayBlockingQueue is bounded (backpressure), LinkedBlockingQueue is optionally bounded, PriorityBlockingQueue is unbounded priority-based. Eliminates explicit wait/notify synchronization for producer-consumer.
keyPoints: put(e): blocks if full (capacity reached) — natural backpressure
take(): blocks if empty — no busy-wait
offer(e, timeout): non-blocking with timeout, returns false if not inserted
poll(timeout): returns null if empty after timeout
SynchronousQueue: zero capacity — put blocks until take is called (handoff)

questions.question: How do Collectors.groupingBy, partitioningBy, and toMap work in streams?
questions.quickAnswer: groupingBy(classifier) partitions elements into a Map<K, List<V>> by key. partitioningBy(predicate) is a special case producing Map<Boolean, List<T>>. toMap(keyFn, valueFn) builds a Map but throws on duplicate keys — use the merge function overload to handle duplicates. Downstream collectors enable aggregation: groupingBy(category, counting()).
keyPoints: groupingBy with downstream: groupingBy(dept, summingInt(Employee::salary))
toMap merge: toMap(key, val, (v1,v2) -> v1) keeps first on duplicate
Collectors.toUnmodifiableMap, toUnmodifiableList (Java 10+)
collectingAndThen: wraps result after collection (e.g., make list unmodifiable)
teeing (Java 12): collect into two collectors simultaneously, merge results
